toxicity_notes: Fruit edible and widely cultivated as a food crop; cultivated varieties are safe. Wild/highly stressed plants can accumulate cucurbitacins producing dangerous bitterness; discard any that taste unusually bitter — especially seeds/skin.
Fruit edible and widely cultivated; safe in cultivated varieties. Wild forms or plants under stress can produce cucurbitacin-induced bitterness causing GI distress; discard bitter fruit.
Edible fruit widely used as a food crop; safe in cultivated forms. As with other Cucurbita, wild/highly stressed vines can accumulate cucurbitacins — avoid consuming bitter-tasting fruit or pulp.
Fruit edible and non-toxic when cultivated; commonly eaten raw or pickled. Vine foliage contains cucurbitacins in wild/stressed plants and can cause mild irritation; fruit is safe.
Fruit edible and non-toxic; a major food crop. Foliage under stress may contain cucurbitacins; fruit of cultivated varieties safe for normal consumption.

Fruit pulp is safe and widely eaten; seeds edible when roasted. Wild/bitter forms of Citrullus (e.g., Citrullus colocynthis) contain cucurbitacins and are toxic — cultivated watermelon varieties are non-toxic.

Young fruit is edible; mature fruit is typically too fibrous/bitter for eating and used instead as containers/utensils. Bitter forms contain cucurbitacins and are toxic — do not consume bitter mature fruit.
Young fruit is edible and eaten as a vegetable; mature fruit becomes fibrous and is used as a natural loofah sponge. Bitter mature fruit contains cucurbitacins — only consume young tender fruit.

Fruit is edible and widely used in Asian cuisines; unripe fruit is intensely bitter. In large amounts fruit can be toxic to pets/humans and is contraindicated in pregnancy; seeds are toxic if ingested in quantity. GI upset, hypoglycemia risk in sensitive individuals.
Fruit, stems, and leaves are edible and widely cultivated as a food crop; generally considered non-toxic and safe. Tubers also edible. No significant documented toxicity.
Fruit is edible and widely used in Asian cuisines; mature fruit stores well. Young shoots and leaves also eaten as vegetables. Generally non-toxic when prepared normally.
Fruit is edible and traditionally used as a vegetable/stuffed dish in Andean cuisine; generally non-toxic. Raw seeds contain saponins and should generally be removed before cooking/eating.

Fruit and young shoots are edible and consumed in tropical Asian/African cuisine; generally non-toxic when prepared. Documented invasive species in tropical regions including the Caribbean, Florida, Hawaii, and Pacific islands.

All parts are toxic, especially the berries and root; contains cucurbitacins and bryonin. Berries are particularly hazardous to children and pets. Poisoning causes GI distress. Skin contact with sap may cause irritation.

All parts are toxic; contains elaterin and cucurbitacins causing severe GI distress. The explosively dehiscent fruit can cause eye/skin irritation on contact. All parts should be handled with care; berries especially dangerous to children and pets.
